activities_projects.title: Reactivity Series: Metal-Salt Displacement
activities_projects.objective: Compare the reactivity of Fe, Zn, Cu, and Al through displacement reactions
steps: Set up four test tubes, each containing a different salt solution
Dip a metal into each solution (e.g., Iron nail in $CuSO_4$, Copper in $FeSO_4$)
Wait for 15-20 minutes and observe if a reaction (color change or coating) occurs
Record which metals successfully displaced others from their salt solutions
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Arranges the given metals in decreasing order of their reactivity based on experimental evidence
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct identification of the displacement reactions and the resulting reactivity sequence (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Amphoteric Oxides Investigation
activities_projects.objective: Demonstrate that some metal oxides react with both acids and bases
steps: Take a small amount of $Al_2O_3$ in two separate test tubes
Add dilute $HCl$ to the first tube and shake well; observe if it dissolves
Add $NaOH$ solution to the second tube and heat gently; observe if it dissolves
Write the chemical equations for both reactions
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Defines amphoteric oxides and identifies Aluminum oxide as a prime example
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct chemical equations for the formation of Aluminum chloride and Sodium aluminate (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Reaction of Metals with Steam
activities_projects.objective: Observe the reaction of less reactive metals with water in gaseous form
steps: Place the soaked glass wool at the end of the tube and the metal in the middle
Heat the metal and then heat the glass wool to produce steam
Pass the steam over the hot metal and collect the resulting gas over water
Test the collected gas with a burning matchstick
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that metals like Al, Fe, and Zn do not react with cold/hot water but react with steam
activities_projects.evaluation: Explanation of the gas collection method and the identity of the gas evolved (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Nature of Non-metal Oxides
activities_projects.objective: Test the acidity of oxides formed by non-metals
steps: Heat a small amount of sulfur in a deflagrating spoon until it burns
Introduce the spoon into a glass jar and cover it with a lid to trap the fumes
Add a little water to the jar and shake to dissolve the gas
Test the solution with blue litmus paper
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Concludes that non-metal oxides (like $SO_2$) are generally acidic and turn blue litmus red
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct identification of the acid formed (Sulfurous acid) and the litmus result (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Properties of Ionic Compounds
activities_projects.objective: Investigate melting point, solubility, and conductivity of salts
steps: Try heating a small amount of salt on a spatula to observe its melting behavior
Check the solubility of the salts in water and in kerosene
Test the electrical conductivity of the solid salt vs. the salt solution
Summarize the findings in a table
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies the high melting point, water solubility, and electrolytic nature of ionic compounds
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of the properties table for the tested ionic compounds (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Reaction of Metals with Nitric Acid
activities_projects.objective: Understand why $H_2$ is usually not evolved with $HNO_3$
steps: Add dilute $HNO_3$ to Zinc and observe if gas is evolved (explain why it forms water instead)
Add very dilute $HNO_3$ to Magnesium and Manganese
Observe the bubbles of gas in the $Mg$ and $Mn$ tubes
Perform the 'pop' test for these specific metals
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes that $HNO_3$ is a strong oxidizing agent, but $Mg$ and $Mn$ can still produce $H_2$ with very dilute acid
activities_projects.evaluation: Explanation of the oxidizing nature of $HNO_3$ vs. the behavior of $Mg/Mn$ (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Formation of Magnesium Chloride
activities_projects.objective: Model the transfer of electrons in ionic bond formation
steps: Write the electronic configuration of Magnesium ($Z=12$) and Chlorine ($Z=17$)
Draw the electron-dot structures for both atoms
Show the transfer of two electrons from one Mg atom to two Cl atoms
Write the resulting ions ($Mg^{2+}$ and $Cl^-$) and the final formula
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Visualizes the formation of ionic bonds through the octet rule
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct representation of the electron-dot diagram and ionic charges (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Aqua Regia Challenge
activities_projects.objective: Research the solvent that dissolves noble metals
steps: Find the ratio of concentrated $HCl$ and concentrated $HNO_3$ used to make Aqua Regia
Research why gold dissolves in this mixture but not in the individual acids
List the safety precautions required when handling this highly corrosive fuming liquid
Identify which other noble metal (e.g., Platinum) can be dissolved by it
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the chemical composition and extreme reactivity of 'Royal Water'
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct ratio ($3:1$) and explanation of the solvent's power (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Electrolytic Refining Model
activities_projects.objective: Understand the process of purifying impure metals
steps: Set up the electrolytic cell with CuSO₄ as the electrolyte
Connect the impure copper to the positive terminal and pure copper to the negative
Pass current for 15 minutes
Observe the deposition on the cathode and the 'anode mud' at the bottom
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Explains how electrolysis is used to obtain 99.9% pure metals in industry
activities_projects.evaluation: Labelled diagram of the electrolytic refining process and ionic equations (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Prevention of Corrosion: Galvanization vs. Painting
activities_projects.objective: Test different protective coatings on iron
steps: Leave one nail plain, paint the second, and use a galvanized third nail
Place all three in beakers containing salt water for 3 days
Observe which nail shows the most and least signs of rusting
Discuss the 'Sacrificial Protection' provided by Zinc
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates the efficiency of different corrosion prevention methods
activities_projects.evaluation: Comparison report on the state of the three nails after the experiment (10 marks)
